mega.desc: Travel to China without a visa! Learn about China\'s 240-hour transit visa-free policy, unilateral visa-free countries, and mutual visa exemption countries.
mega.title: Visa-Free Entry to China

mutual.desc: Citizens of both countries holding ordinary passports can usually travel to each other\'s country for short term without prior visa application.
mutual.title: List of Countries with Mutual Visa-Free Agreements

sidebar.portDesc1: Eligible: Citizens of 54 countries (e.g., US, UK, Japan)
sidebar.portDesc2: Requirement: Only available at 60 designated ports like Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou.
sidebar.areaDesc1: Allowed: Travel or activities within 24 provinces/municipalities including Beijing, Shanghai, Sichuan.
sidebar.areaDesc2: Prohibited: Staying outside the prescribed area.
sidebar.ruleTitle: Compliance: Strict Adherence to Time & Registration
sidebar.ruleDesc1: Time: Max stay 240h (starting from 00:00 the day after entry)
sidebar.ruleDesc2: Overstay: Must apply for residence permit in advance (only for emergencies/force majeure).
sidebar.ruleDesc3: Registration: Register accommodation within 24h (hotel or police station).
sidebar.ruleDesc4: Restricted: Only for short-term tourism/business; prohibited from working/studying.
sidebar.disclaimer: Disclaimer: Information provided is for reference only. For specific policies, please refer to the official NIA website. Policies subject to change.
sidebar.materialDesc2: Confirmed connecting ticket (flying to a third country within 240h, cannot return to origin)
sidebar.materialDesc3: Process: Submit documents at border inspection on arrival; entry granted after approval.

section.desc: China has expanded the 144-hour and newly introduced 240-hour transit visa-free policies to multiple countries. Eligible foreign travelers and business people can enter through designated ports and stay in designated areas for up to 6–10 days without prior visa application.
section.title: About the 240-Hour Transit Visa-Free Policy

unilateral.desc: Citizens of the following countries holding ordinary passports can enter China without a visa for tourism, business, and visiting relatives.
unilateral.title: List of Countries with Unilateral Visa-Free Entry

fee.reciprocalDesc: For countries that have mutual visa exemption agreements with China, visa fees for their citizens may be charged according to the agreement. Please refer to the announcements of the Chinese embassies and consulates.
fee.expedited: Expedited Service Fee: Additional fee of approximately 200-500 CNY for expedited processing
fee.mailing: Mailing Fee: Courier fee of approximately 20-50 CNY for mailing visa materials or passport
fee.medical: Medical Examination Fee: Required for certain visa types (e.g., Work Visa Z, Long-term Study Visa X1), approximately 400-800 CNY
fee.permit1: Residence Permit Application Fee: approximately 800 CNY/year (applicable to long-term residence visas such as Z, X1, Q1, S1)
fee.permit2: Residence Permit Extension Fee: approximately 800 CNY/time
fee.permit3: Residence Permit Change Fee: approximately 200 CNY/time (e.g., changing residence purpose, address, etc.)
fee.feeNote1: The above fees are for reference only. Actual fees may vary depending on nationality, application location, number of entries, and other factors. Please refer to the latest standards published by the Chinese embassy or consulate in your area.
fee.feeNote2: Visa fees are usually collected in local currency, and exchange rate fluctuations may affect the actual payment amount.

type.tourism.desc: China Tourism Visa (L Visa) is for persons traveling to China for tourism. Round-trip air tickets and hotel reservations are required.
type.business.desc: Business Visa (M Visa) is for persons traveling to China for commercial and trade activities.
type.familyQ1.desc: Q1 Visa is for persons who will reside in China for family reunion purposes for more than 180 days.
type.familyQ2.desc: Q2 Visa is for persons who will stay in China for a short-term family visit for no more than 180 days.
type.work.desc: Work Visa (Z Visa) is for persons who will work in China. A work permit is required.
type.studyX1.desc: X1 Visa is for persons who will study in China for more than 180 days.
type.studyX2.desc: X2 Visa is for persons who will study in China for no more than 180 days.
type.transit.desc: G Visa is for persons who will transit through China to a third country.
type.crew.desc: C Visa is for crew members of international transportation vehicles performing duties such as crew, aviation, or shipping.
type.permanent.desc: D Visa is for foreign nationals applying for permanent residence in China.
type.exchange.desc: F Visa is for persons traveling to China for exchange, visits, study tours, and other non-commercial activities.
type.journalistJ1.desc: J1 Visa is for foreign journalists of foreign news organizations stationed in China for more than 180 days.
type.journalistJ2.desc: J2 Visa is for foreign journalists who will cover temporary news events in China.
type.talent.desc: R Visa is for foreign high-level talents and urgently needed professional talents required by China.
type.privateS1.desc: S1 Visa is for spouses, parents, etc. of foreigners staying in China for work, study, etc., for more than 180 days.
type.privateS2.desc: S2 Visa is for short-term family visits and other private affairs purposes.

doc.passport.desc: Valid passport with at least 6 months validity and blank visa pages. Original and copy required.
doc.formAndPhoto.desc: Completed PRC Visa Application Form, one recent color photo with white background (48mm×33mm).
doc.residence.desc: If applying from a country other than your nationality, provide valid legal residence or visa proof for that country.
doc.previous.desc: If you were previously Chinese citizen or changed passports, provide former passport or old visa copies.
doc.L.itinerary.desc: Round-trip flight booking and hotel reservation
doc.L.invitation.desc: Invitation letter from a Chinese entity or individual
doc.M.invitation.desc: Invitation letter from a trading company in China (company seal required)
doc.M.business.desc: Company business license or introduction letter
doc.M.bank.desc: Bank statement for the past 6 months
doc.Q1.invitation.desc: Issued by a Chinese citizen or permanent resident
doc.Q1.inviterId.desc: Copy of the inviter's identity document
doc.Q2.invitation.desc: Invitation letter and copy of inviter's identity document
doc.Z.workPermit.desc: Foreign Work Permit Notice obtained by Chinese employer
doc.Z.noCriminal.desc: No criminal record certificate certified by Chinese embassy
doc.Z.health.desc: Health examination certificate certified by Chinese embassy
doc.X1.health.desc: Health examination certificate certified by Chinese embassy
doc.D.confirmForm.desc: Foreign Residence Identity Confirmation Form issued by Ministry of Public Security
doc.D.criminal.desc: Foreign national criminal record certificate (must be authenticated)
doc.D.health.desc: Health examination certificate from a recognized hospital in China
doc.F.invitation.desc: Invitation letter from Chinese inviting organization or individual
doc.F.plan.desc: Study or exchange plan in China
doc.J1.notification.desc: Notice letter from Information Department of Ministry of Foreign Affairs
doc.S1.family.desc: Family relationship proof with person staying in China
doc.S1.residentDocs.desc: Copies of passport and residence permit of person staying in China
doc.S1.invitation.desc: Invitation letter from person staying in China
doc.S2.family.desc: Family relationship proof with person in China

faq.q1: How does tripcngo.com generate dish images?
faq.a1: Our AI system analyzes the text description from the menu, cross-references it with a vast culinary database, and uses generative AI to match or create high-fidelity dish images for you.
faq.q2: Can I use it for non-Chinese menus?
faq.a2: Currently, we focus primarily on Chinese-to-English translation, but our system supports character recognition for multiple major languages.
faq.a3: Yes, we integrate with a real-time exchange rate API to provide accurate conversion between CNY and major foreign currencies based on latest rates.
faq.q4: Can I save my menu recognition records?
faq.a4: Registered users can view their full recognition history, including translations and generated images, in their personal dashboard.
faq.q5: Why are some dishes not recognized correctly?
faq.a5: Artistic fonts or messy handwriting can reduce accuracy. For best results, ensure good lighting and keep the menu flat when capturing.
faq.q6: How long does it take to process a menu?
faq.a6: Typically, the entire process of scanning, translating, and image generation takes only 10-15 seconds.
faq.q7: Do I need an internet connection?
faq.a7: Yes, the AI processing and image generation run in the cloud, so a stable internet connection is required.
faq.q8: How is tripcngo.com different from standard translators?
faq.a8: We go beyond text translation by providing a visual experience, allowing you to see what the dish looks like alongside currency conversions.

pinyin.desc: Convert Chinese to Pinyin, smart segmentation, and stroke order animation
pinyin.title: Chinese to Pinyin & Segmentation
pinyin.inputPlaceholder: Enter Chinese sentence to start analyzing...
pinyin.clickCharHint: 💡 Click any character to view stroke order animation

guide.heroDesc: Our tool helps you convert any Chinese sentence into standard Pinyin, perform smart segmentation, view stroke order animations for each character, and get accurate English translations. Whether you're learning Chinese, studying linguistics, or processing Chinese text, this tool provides comprehensive language analysis.
guide.featurePinyinDesc: Convert Chinese characters to standard Pinyin with complete tone marks to help you pronounce accurately.
guide.featureSegmentDesc: Automatically break Chinese sentences into meaningful word units, each with Pinyin and English definitions.
guide.featureStrokeDesc: Click any character to view its stroke order animation and learn the correct writing sequence.
guide.featureTranslateDesc: Provide accurate English translations for the entire sentence to help understand the overall meaning.
guide.featureVoiceDesc: Support speech synthesis to hear standard Chinese pronunciation for assisted learning.
guide.whatIsPinyinDesc: Hanyu Pinyin is the official romanization system for Standard Chinese. It was developed in the 1950s and adopted by the Chinese government in 1958. It uses the Latin alphabet to represent the pronunciation of Chinese characters, making it easier for non-native speakers to learn and pronounce correctly.
guide.toneIntro: Chinese is a tonal language with four main tones and one neutral tone. Each tone is represented by a different mark above the vowel.
guide.tone1Desc: High-level — tone stays high and flat
guide.tone2Desc: Rising — tone rises from low to high
guide.tone4Desc: Falling — tone drops sharply from high to low
guide.toneNeutralDesc: Light and short tone, usually unmarked
guide.step1: Pinyin Conversion: Convert Chinese characters to standard Pinyin with complete tone marks for accurate pronunciation.
guide.step2: Smart Segmentation: Automatically break Chinese sentences into meaningful word units with Pinyin and English definitions.
guide.step3: Stroke Order Animation: Click any character to view its stroke order animation and learn the correct writing sequence.
guide.step4: English Translation: Get accurate English translations for the entire sentence to understand the overall meaning.
guide.step5: Voice Playback: Use speech synthesis to hear standard Chinese pronunciation for assisted learning.
guide.segmentDesc: Chinese segmentation is the process of splitting a continuous sequence of Chinese characters into meaningful words. Unlike English, Chinese text has no obvious word boundaries, making segmentation a crucial step in understanding Chinese. The tool recognizes complete words in sentences, such as "图书馆" (library) and "学习" (study), rather than individual characters. Each word can be further broken down into individual characters, each with its own Pinyin, tone, and definition.
guide.segmentExampleDesc: "我想去图书馆" would be segmented into "我" (I), "想" (want), "去" (go), "图书馆" (library).
guide.strokeDesc: Stroke order refers to the sequence in which strokes are written when forming Chinese characters. Mastering correct stroke order not only makes writing more beautiful but also improves speed and accuracy.
guide.strokeRule1: Basic rules: top to bottom, left to right, horizontal before vertical, left-falling before right-falling.
guide.strokeRule2: Grid practice: Our tool provides a grid background to help you understand character structure and positioning.
guide.strokeRule3: Animation demo: Click a character to watch the stroke order animation and learn the correct writing sequence intuitively.
guide.faq1Q: Why are tone marks important in Pinyin?
guide.faq1A: Tone marks are crucial because they indicate the pitch pattern of each syllable. In Chinese, the same syllable pronounced with different tones can have completely different meanings. For example, the syllable "ma" can mean: mā (mother), má (hemp), mǎ (horse), mà (scold).
guide.faq2Q: Why is segmentation needed? Is it accurate?
guide.faq2A: Chinese segmentation is fundamental to understanding Chinese text. Our tool uses advanced AI models for smart segmentation, capable of recognizing common word combinations and language patterns. While segmentation is accurate in most cases, some ambiguous sentences may have different valid segmentations. Our tool provides the most natural segmentation results.
guide.faq3Q: How do I view detailed character information?
guide.faq3A: In the segmentation results, click any Chinese character to open a detail window. The detail window includes: complete Pinyin and tone, English definition and explanation, stroke order animation, and voice playback function.
guide.faq4Q: Can all Chinese characters be converted to Pinyin?
guide.faq4A: All standard Chinese characters have corresponding Pinyin. However, some characters (polyphonic characters) may have different pronunciations depending on context. Our tool selects the most appropriate pronunciation based on context, and for specific scenarios like names, it prioritizes common pronunciations.
guide.faq5Q: What types of input does the tool support?
guide.faq5A: Our tool supports any Chinese text input, including: Chinese sentences and paragraphs, Chinese names, text with punctuation, and mixed Chinese-English content. We recommend input length not exceeding 120 characters for the best analysis results.

zodiac.desc: Calculate your Chinese zodiac sign and learn its cultural meaning.
zodiac.result: Result: Born in {year}, your zodiac sign is:
zodiac.heroDesc: Check your Chinese zodiac sign by birthday, learn about its culture and travel inspiration.
zodiac.benmingDesc: Benming year is the year that shares your birth zodiac sign. Many people consider it a year to be more cautious and mindful of life changes, and wear red for good luck and protection.
zodiac.footerNote: This result is calculated based on the Chinese New Year date of your birth year.
zodiac.faq1Title: When does the zodiac year change?
zodiac.faq1P1: 2026 is the Year of the Horse in the Chinese zodiac, but the zodiac year doesn't start on January 1st.
zodiac.faq1P2: The 2026 Chinese New Year starts on February 17, 2026. The Horse is associated with vitality, freedom, action, and confidence.
zodiac.faq1P3: In the Five Elements system, this year is also called the "Fire Horse." In the traditional Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches system, 2026 is known as the "Fire Horse Year."
zodiac.faq1P4: If you want to plan a trip with a zodiac theme, focus on vibrant cities, open landscapes, historical routes, and Chinese New Year festivities.
zodiac.nextPreviewDesc: 2027 will enter the Year of the Goat, often associated with gentleness, aesthetics, creativity, and a soft temperament.
zodiac.detailDesc: Select a zodiac sign to view personality traits, emotional compatibility, and travel vibes.
zodiac.howDesc: Understand the basic rules and mechanics of the Chinese zodiac
zodiac.howStep1Title: Your zodiac sign is determined by your birth year
zodiac.howStep1Desc: The Chinese zodiac is based on the lunar calendar year, with each year corresponding to a zodiac animal. Your sign depends on the year you were born — specifically, the year after the Chinese New Year. For example, those born after February 17, 2026 are Horses, while those born before are Snakes.
zodiac.howStep2Desc: The twelve zodiac signs cycle in a fixed order: Rat, Ox, Tiger, Rabbit, Dragon, Snake, Horse, Goat, Monkey, Rooster, Dog, and Pig. Every 12 years completes one full cycle, then it starts over. This means every 12 years you enter your benmingnian (zodiac year).
zodiac.howStep3Title: Five Elements overlay with zodiac signs
zodiac.howStep3Desc: Each zodiac year also corresponds to one of the Five Elements: Metal, Wood, Water, Fire, or Earth. The same zodiac sign has 5 elemental variants (e.g., 'Fire Horse' vs. 'Wood Horse'), making the complete zodiac-element cycle 60 years. 2026 is the 'Fire Horse' year.
zodiac.travelTitle: How to Experience Zodiac Culture When Traveling in China
zodiac.travelDesc: Zodiac culture is everywhere in Chinese daily life. Here are some ways to experience it during your trip
zodiac.tip1Desc: Chinese New Year (Spring Festival) is the most important holiday in China. You'll find zodiac-themed decorations, lantern festivals, and celebrations everywhere. Visiting during this time lets you experience the vibrant zodiac culture firsthand.
zodiac.tip2Desc: Many palaces, temples, and ancient buildings feature zodiac sculptures and murals. For example, the Forbidden City and Temple of Heaven in Beijing are filled with zodiac elements.
zodiac.tip3Desc: During your trip to China, you can purchase zodiac-related souvenirs such as zodiac stamps, figurines, and charms — a uniquely Chinese way to commemorate your visit.
zodiac.tip4Desc: At jewelry stores and markets across China, you'll find zodiac-themed accessories like jade pendants, bracelets, and necklaces. Many people choose to wear their zodiac sign for peace and good fortune.
zodiac.legendDesc: The classic tale of how the Jade Emperor selected the twelve zodiac animals — see how each one crossed the finish line
zodiac.faqSectionDesc: Quick answers for travelers encountering Chinese zodiac culture for the first time.
zodiac.faqQ1: What is my Chinese zodiac sign?
zodiac.faqA1: Your Chinese zodiac sign depends on your birth year. Each lunar year corresponds to a zodiac animal in a 12-year cycle. Use the calculator at the top of this page to find your sign by entering your birth year.
zodiac.faqQ2: How is the Chinese zodiac calculated?
zodiac.faqA2: The Chinese zodiac is based on the lunar calendar with a 12-year cycle. Each year corresponds to an animal: Rat, Ox, Tiger, Rabbit, Dragon, Snake, Horse, Goat, Monkey, Rooster, Dog, and Pig. The calculation uses your birth year modulo 12 to determine the corresponding animal.
zodiac.faqQ3: Does the Chinese zodiac change on January 1st?
zodiac.faqA3: No. The Chinese zodiac switches at Chinese New Year (Spring Festival), which falls between late January and mid-February. For example, in 2026, Chinese New Year is on February 17 — those born before this date belong to the previous sign (Snake), while those born after belong to the new sign (Horse).
zodiac.faqQ4: What is the zodiac animal for 2026?
zodiac.faqA4: 2026 is the Year of the Horse (Fire Horse), starting from February 17, 2026 (Chinese New Year) and ending on February 6, 2027. The Horse symbolizes vitality, freedom, and success in Chinese culture.
zodiac.faqQ5: What is the order of the twelve zodiac animals?
zodiac.faqA5: The fixed order is: Rat, Ox, Tiger, Rabbit, Dragon, Snake, Horse, Goat, Monkey, Rooster, Dog, and Pig. This order comes from the legendary race organized by the Jade Emperor, where animals ranked by their finishing order.
zodiac.faqQ6: Is the Chinese zodiac the same as Western astrology?
zodiac.faqA6: No. The Chinese zodiac operates on a 12-year cycle based on birth year and the Spring Festival, while Western zodiac signs are divided by calendar dates within a single year. Both have 12 symbols, but the systems, calculation methods, and cultural meanings are entirely different.
zodiac.faqQ7: Is the Chinese zodiac scientifically based?
zodiac.faqA7: The Chinese zodiac belongs to traditional culture and folklore, not modern science. It originated from ancient observations and imagination about time and nature, carrying rich cultural symbolism and folk wisdom. Understanding the zodiac is more about appreciating Chinese culture than scientific prediction.
zodiac.faqQ8: Where can I see zodiac culture while traveling?
zodiac.faqA8: You can experience zodiac culture throughout China: zodiac decorations at Spring Festival temple fairs and lantern festivals, zodiac sculptures at historic sites like the Forbidden City, zodiac blessing areas in temples, zodiac-themed souvenirs and stamps in gift shops, and zodiac exhibitions in museums.
zodiac.faqQ9: What is the relationship between the Chinese zodiac and Spring Festival?
zodiac.faqA9: The zodiac and Spring Festival are inseparable. Each Spring Festival marks the beginning of a new zodiac year, and the current year's zodiac animal appears everywhere — from red envelopes and lanterns to window decorations and TV gala mascots. Spring Festival is also when people pay special attention to their benmingnian (zodiac year), often wearing red clothing for good luck and protection.

faq1.desc: The zodiac sign does not change on January 1st, but on the Chinese New Year (Lunar New Year).
faq1.title: When does the zodiac year change?

faq2.desc: There are 12 signs: Rat, Ox, Tiger, Rabbit, Dragon, Snake, Horse, Goat, Monkey, Rooster, Dog, and Pig.
faq2.title: Twelve Zodiac Signs

zodiac.title: Calculate your Chinese zodiac sign and learn its cultural meaning.
zodiac.legendText1: The clever Rat rode on the Ox's back and jumped ahead at the river crossing, finishing first.
zodiac.legendText2: The hardworking Ox would have been first, but the Rat hitched a ride across the river, making Ox second.
zodiac.legendText3: The powerful Tiger struggled against the river currents, arriving in third place after much effort.
zodiac.legendText4: The agile Rabbit hopped across the river using stepping stones and driftwood, earning fourth place.
zodiac.legendText5: The Dragon could have easily been first, but stopped to bring rain to a drought-stricken village, arriving fifth.
zodiac.legendText6: The Snake hid near the Horse's hooves to cross the river, suddenly appearing at the last moment, finishing sixth.
zodiac.legendText7: The galloping Horse was startled by the suddenly appearing Snake, hesitated briefly, and arrived seventh.
zodiac.legendText8: The gentle Goat found a raft with the Monkey and Rooster, helping each other across the river together.
zodiac.legendText9: The clever Monkey worked with the Goat and Rooster to paddle the raft, arriving ninth through teamwork.
zodiac.legendText10: The Rooster found the raft and invited the Goat and Monkey along, arriving tenth with their help.
zodiac.legendText11: The Dog was a fast runner but had too much fun playing in the river, forgetting the race, arriving eleventh.
zodiac.legendText12: The hungry Pig stopped for a snack along the way and even took a nap, arriving last.

guide.welcomeTitle: Welcome to the Professional Chinese Character Counter
guide.welcomeDesc: This tool provides precise text data analysis. Enter text in the box to get real-time statistics on the following:
guide.statEnglish: English characters: counts all Latin letters (upper & lower case)
guide.copyHint: After editing, click the "Copy" button to copy all content for easy pasting, or click the "Clear" button to empty the text box.
guide.hanziTitle: Introduction to the Chinese Writing System
guide.hanziDesc: Chinese characters (hanzi) form a writing system rooted in pictography. Early characters were pictographic in nature, and some still retain visual cues, though they differ from pure ideograms. For example, the character "姓" (surname) combines "女" (woman) and "生" (birth), signifying descent through the maternal line.
guide.structureDesc: Chinese has no alphabet. Its basic units are individual characters composed of radicals and strokes, which combine into words and sentences. There are over 100,000 hanzi in total; mastering about 3,000 suffices for daily communication, 5,000 indicates a good education, and 8,000+ suggests scholarly proficiency.
guide.structureDesc2: Hanzi structures are diverse: semi-enclosed forms like "巨", fully enclosed forms like "国", simple characters like "人", and compound characters like "森", "猫", and "孬".
guide.meaningDesc: Some hanzi allow you to infer meaning or pronunciation from their components. For instance, "猫" (cat): the "犭" radical signals an animal, while "苗" suggests the pronunciation (miáo); together they denote a feline. "孬" combines "不" (not) and "好" (good), directly meaning "bad".
guide.meaningDesc2: However, such patterns are not universal. "裸" is not read as "果" (guǒ) but as "luǒ"; characters like "蜀" and "侪" are commonly misread, even by native speakers.
guide.pinyinTitle: The Pinyin System and Its Role
guide.pinyinDesc: Pinyin, developed under the leadership of scholar Zhou Youguang, uses the Latin alphabet to transcribe Chinese pronunciation. It comprises three elements: initials (shengmu), finals (yunmu), and tones. The current system has 23 initials, 24 finals, and four tone marks (ˉ, ˊ, ˇ, ˋ). For example, in "树" (shù), "sh" is the initial, "u" is the final, and "ˋ" marks the falling tone.
guide.pinyinDesc2: Tones directly affect meaning: "mā" (mother) and "má" (hemp) differ in meaning solely due to tone.
guide.footer: Thank you for using this tool. If you have questions or feedback, please feel free to contact us.

story.p1: The birth of tripcngo.com stems from our passion for China and obsession with the travel experience.
story.p2: We believe travel should be spontaneous and full of surprises, not just checking in at popular spots.
story.p3: With the 240-hour transit visa-free policy, more friends are visiting China. However, challenges like language, internet, and payments can be daunting.
story.p4: So we created tripcngo.com to bridge the information gap, combining our travel expertise with AI technology.
story.p5: From visa guides to smart helpers, we help you discover the real and beautiful China.

intro.p1: The People\'s Republic of China is one of the oldest countries in the world, with 5,000 years of civilization.
intro.p2: Located in East Asia, China was founded in 1949. It is one of the world\'s most populous countries with 1.4 billion people and an area of 9.6 million sq km (3rd largest).
intro.p3: The terrain follows a three-step ladder from the high Qinghai-Tibet Plateau in the west to the low plains and coastline in the east. This fall creates diverse landscapes including deserts, plateaus, and basins.
intro.p4: China is divided into 23 provinces, 5 autonomous regions, 4 municipalities (Beijing, Shanghai, Tianjin, Chongqing), and 2 SARs (Hong Kong, Macau).
intro.p5: Unified multi-ethnic country with 56 ethnic groups. Han is the majority. Mandarin is the common language.
intro.p6: Chinese culture is profound and colorful. It holds a significant position in world culture. Traditional arts include poetry, opera, calligraphy, and painting.
intro.title: Intro to China

hero.desc: Explore the most popular cities in China. Discover what\'s fun in Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, and find top food and shopping recommendations.
